title: Microsoft + Activision, One Year Later: What the $69B Deal Actually Changed
subtitle: Game Pass grew, Call of Duty stayed on PlayStation, and Bobby Kotick is long gone. Here's the honest scorecard.
excerpt: It was the biggest gaming acquisition in history. Twelve months in, we take stock of what Microsoft got, what it gave up, and whether the deal made strategic sense.
author: riley-chen
seo_title: Microsoft Activision Acquisition: One Year Later Review
seo_description: A thorough review of what changed — and what didn't — in the year since Microsoft closed the $69 billion Activision Blizzard acquisition.
body:
The Deal That Took Two Years to Close

After navigating regulatory challenges in the UK, EU, and US, Microsoft closed its $68.7 billion acquisition of Activision Blizzard King in October 2023. The deal, first announced in January 2022, became the defining story of a pivotal moment in gaming's consolidation era.

Twelve months on, it's worth stepping back and asking: what actually changed?

Game Pass: Meaningful Growth, But Not a Step Change

The most explicit rationale for the acquisition was Game Pass content. Titles like Call of Duty, World of Warcraft, Diablo, and Overwatch on Game Pass would give Microsoft a compelling subscription library that Netflix-style competes for gaming time. The reality is more nuanced. Call of Duty did arrive on Game Pass, driving what Microsoft called the "biggest day" in Game Pass history. But subscriber numbers remain tightly held, and third-party estimates suggest growth of roughly 20–30% over the year — meaningful, not transformational.

Platform Exclusivity: The Concessions Were Real

To get the deal done, Microsoft made binding 10-year commitments to keep Call of Duty on PlayStation, commitments it also extended to Nintendo and other platforms. This fundamentally limits exclusivity as a competitive weapon for the franchise. Whether this was a reasonable price for closing the deal or a strategic error that undermined the acquisition's logic is a legitimate debate.

The Studio Health Question

Activision Blizzard's internal culture was well-documented as troubled before the acquisition. Since closing, Microsoft has conducted significant layoffs across the combined gaming organization — over 1,900 jobs cut in January 2024 alone. Several studio leaders departed. The integration work is ongoing and it will take years to evaluate whether Microsoft has improved or maintained the creative output of its new studios.

Net Assessment

The deal made sense on paper: durable franchises, mobile (King's Candy Crush), and Game Pass content. The execution challenges are real but not existential. Microsoft is playing a very long game, and 12 months is too short a window to render a final verdict.
